title: SEAI Better Energy Homes 2025: Complete Grant Guide
author: RetrofitHub Editorial Team
summary: Everything you need to know about SEAI's Better Energy Homes scheme — grant amounts, eligible works, how to apply, and how to find an approved contractor.
body: The SEAI Better Energy Homes scheme is Ireland's flagship residential energy efficiency programme, offering homeowners grants of up to €25,000 for a range of energy upgrade measures. In 2025, the scheme continues to be one of the most generous home improvement subsidy programmes in Europe.

Eligible works under the scheme include attic insulation (up to €1,500), wall insulation (up to €8,000 for external or internal), heat pumps (up to €6,500), solar PV panels (up to €2,400), and heating controls upgrades. If you carry out two or more measures, you may also qualify for an additional €2,000 bonus payment.

To qualify, your home must have been built and occupied before 2011, and you must use an SEAI registered contractor for all works. The grant is paid directly to the contractor, so you only pay the balance after the works are completed and the grant is approved.

The application process has been streamlined in recent years. You apply online at seai.ie, receive grant approval, hire an approved contractor, have the works completed, and then submit your completion documentation. Most applications are processed within four to six weeks.

One important change for 2025 is the introduction of an income-based top-up grant for households earning under €50,000 per year. These households can receive an additional 20% on eligible measures, making the scheme even more accessible.

To find a registered contractor near you, use the directory on this site or search the SEAI contractor database. Always get at least three quotes and ensure your contractor is currently listed on the SEAI register before signing any agreement.

title: How to Insulate Your Attic: A Step-by-Step Guide
author: Ciarán Murphy, BEng
summary: Attic insulation is one of the most cost-effective ways to improve your home's energy rating. This guide walks you through the process from choosing a contractor to what to expect on the day.
body: Attic insulation is consistently rated as one of the best-value energy upgrades you can make to an Irish home. Up to 25% of a home's heat loss occurs through the roof, and in older homes with little or no insulation, upgrading can save hundreds of euro per year on heating bills.

The most common type of attic insulation used in Irish homes is mineral wool (glass or rock wool) laid between and over the joists. The current Irish building regulations require a minimum of 300mm total depth for retrofits, though most professionals recommend 400mm for optimal performance.

Before any work begins, your contractor should inspect the attic for issues such as roof leaks, condensation, or damaged timbers. Any problems should be fixed before insulation is installed, as trapping moisture inside an attic can cause serious long-term damage.

The installation itself is typically completed in one day. Your contractor will also insulate the hatch door, the side walls of any water tank, and any pipes running through the attic space. The water tank itself should not be insulated underneath, as it needs some heat from the room below to prevent freezing.
If you have storage in your attic, your contractor can install a raised platform to allow continued use of the space while maintaining full insulation depth. This costs more but is often worth it.

SEAI grants of up to €1,500 are available for attic insulation through the Better Energy Homes scheme. You must use an SEAI registered contractor and apply for the grant before works begin. Typical project costs range from €800 to €2,000 depending on the size of your attic and access conditions.

title: Local Authority Home Improvement Grants: What's Available in 2025
author: Aoife Brennan
summary: Ireland's local authorities offer a range of home improvement grants that complement SEAI funding. Here's a breakdown of what's available and who qualifies.
body: Alongside the national SEAI schemes, most of Ireland's 31 local authorities offer their own home improvement grants and loan schemes. These can be stacked on top of SEAI grants, significantly reducing the out-of-pocket cost of a retrofit.

The most widely available is the Housing Adaptation Grant for People with a Disability, available from all local authorities and worth up to €30,000. While not exclusively for energy upgrades, many applicants use it to fund heating system improvements where a health need exists.

The Mobility Aids Grant (up to €6,000) and the Housing Aid for Older Persons Grant (up to €8,000) are also available nationally. The older persons grant in particular can cover general repair works including heating, windows, and insulation for those aged 66 and over on low incomes.

Several local authorities also run their own energy efficiency schemes. Dublin City Council, Cork City Council, and Limerick City and County Council have all operated pilot schemes providing subsidised insulation to social housing tenants and in some cases private homeowners in designated areas.

The Sustainable Energy Communities (SEC) programme, run by SEAI but delivered locally, provides funding and support to community groups pursuing whole-area energy upgrades. If your street or estate forms a group, you may be eligible for additional support and a dedicated project manager.

To find out exactly what's available in your county, contact your local authority's housing department directly, or visit your county council's website. Requirements and amounts vary significantly, and many grants are means-tested based on household income.

title: Planning Permission for External Wall Insulation in Ireland
author: Siobhán O'Neill
summary: Do you need planning permission to insulate the outside of your house? The rules changed in 2022 — here's what you need to know before you start.
body: One of the most common questions homeowners ask before starting an external wall insulation (EWI) project is whether they need planning permission. The good news is that for the vast majority of Irish homes, EWI is now classified as exempted development, meaning no planning application is required.

Under the Planning and Development Regulations 2022, external insulation of a house where it does not involve the replacement of windows or doors and the total thickness added does not exceed 250mm is exempted development. This exemption applies to all elevations of a dwelling house, not just the rear.

However, there are important exceptions. If your home is a protected structure or is within an Architectural Conservation Area (ACA), the exemption does not apply, and you will need to seek planning permission. Properties within the curtilage of a protected structure are also affected.

In practice, this means homes in historic town centres, Georgian terraces, and many older rural estates may require planning consent. Your EWI contractor should flag this at survey stage, but it's wise to check your home's planning status on your local authority's planning portal before committing to a project.

Even where planning permission is not required, EWI work must comply with Part L of the Building Regulations (Conservation of Fuel and Energy) and Part B (Fire Safety). Your contractor is legally responsible for compliance with the building regulations, and any SEAI grant-funded work will be inspected.

If you are in any doubt about whether your property requires planning permission, contact your local authority's planning department for pre-application consultation. This is a free service and can save significant time and money down the line.

title: Heat Pump Sales Surge as Ireland Moves Away from Oil and Gas
author: RetrofitHub Editorial Team
summary: Air-to-water heat pump installations in Ireland hit a record high in 2024, driven by rising fossil fuel prices and increased SEAI grant support. We look at what's driving the trend.
body: Heat pump installations in Ireland grew by 38% in 2024 compared to the previous year, according to figures published by the Heat Pump Association of Ireland. Over 18,000 units were installed in residential properties, making it the fastest-growing segment of the home energy upgrade market.

The growth is being driven by a combination of factors: rising gas and oil prices, improved grant support through the SEAI Better Energy Homes scheme (which now offers up to €6,500 for heat pump installation), and a growing network of trained and SEAI-registered installers.

Air-to-water heat pumps, which extract heat from outdoor air and use it to heat water for radiators and underfloor heating, account for the majority of installations. These systems are generally the most cost-effective retrofit option and can work with suitably sized existing radiators in well-insulated homes.

Installer capacity has been a significant constraint in recent years, with long waiting times reported across the industry. However, the SEAI's heat pump installer training programme has expanded significantly, and several new companies have entered the market, helping to reduce lead times from 12–18 months in 2023 to around six months in many areas.

One challenge for the market is public awareness of the conditions required for heat pumps to work effectively. For best results, homes should achieve a BER of C1 or better before installing a heat pump, which often requires prior wall and attic insulation. Heat pumps in poorly insulated homes can be inefficient and expensive to run.

Looking ahead, the government's Climate Action Plan targets 400,000 heat pump installations in existing homes by 2030. Achieving this will require a significant acceleration in the current rate of installation, continued grant support, and ongoing expansion of the installer workforce.

title: What is a One-Stop-Shop Retrofit and Is It Right for You?
author: Pádraig Walsh
summary: The One-Stop-Shop model handles your entire retrofit from first call to BER certificate. But how does it work, and is it better than managing the project yourself?
body: The one-stop-shop (OSS) retrofit model is a relatively new concept in the Irish market, introduced alongside the National Home Energy Upgrade Scheme (NHEUS) in 2022. Rather than hiring multiple contractors and managing your own grant applications, you engage a single registered OSS provider who handles everything — from initial assessment and grant administration to project management and final certification.

The process typically works as follows: you contact an OSS provider, who carries out a whole-home energy assessment. They then produce a tailored retrofit plan showing which measures will achieve the best energy rating improvement, along with a complete cost breakdown. They handle all SEAI grant paperwork on your behalf and coordinate all sub-contractors.

The financial structure of the OSS model is attractive: SEAI pays the grants directly to the OSS provider, and you only pay the balance. This means less upfront outlay and no chasing grant payments. For a typical deep retrofit targeting a B2 BER, the grant can cover 50–80% of total costs depending on the measures involved.

The key advantage over the DIY approach is reduced complexity. Managing multiple contractors — an insulation company, a heat pump installer, a ventilation specialist, a window company — while also submitting grant applications is genuinely time-consuming. The OSS model removes that burden.

However, OSS providers typically charge a project management fee (often 10–15% of total project cost), and you have less direct control over contractor selection. It's important to check what subcontractors they use and ask for references from previous projects.

One-stop-shop retrofit is generally most suitable for homeowners planning a significant upgrade — aiming for a B2 or better rating, involving three or more measures. For smaller jobs like attic insulation alone, the direct SEAI application route with a single contractor is usually simpler and cheaper.
